title: 3D Scanning — Structured Light and Laser Triangulation
content: 3D scanning reconstructs object geometry by rotating the object on a turntable while measuring distance with a laser or structured-light sensor. Point cloud is built from r(θ) measurements; Cartesian conversion: x=r·cos(θ), y=r·sin(θ), z=height.
extended_content: Methods: 1) Laser triangulation — laser line projected onto object, camera captures stripe displacement. Depth resolution ≈ 0.1 mm at 300 mm range. 2) Structured light (e.g. DLP projector + camera) — fringe patterns decode phase → depth map. 3) Time-of-flight (Arduino + VL53L0X TOF sensor + stepper turntable) — low cost, 1–3 mm accuracy. Turntable steps: 200 steps/rev (stepper) → 1.8° resolution → n_points = 200 per layer × n_heights. Scan time: 200 positions × 0.5 s = 100 s per object. Software: OpenSCAD, MeshLab, Point Cloud Library (PCL) for reconstruction. STL mesh from point cloud: Poisson surface reconstruction (>80 000 pts for 0.5 mm resolution). Arduino controls stepper + captures HC-SR04/TOF sensor data via serial to PC.
units: r: mm, θ: deg, x,y,z: mm
source: Levoy, The Digital Michelangelo Project, 2000; Hartley & Zisserman, Multiple View Geometry; VL53L0X Datasheet ST Microelectronics
